one_line_takeaway: The paper's own contribution is a geometry-based (mean-variance manifold) family of column-wise EHR transformation operators (T1 rotation, T2 noise+projection, T3 negative-control reflection, plus a per-stay Q-mix extension) with a formalized no-key/structure-aware threat model and L0-L2/A-D attack taxonomy, empirically showing that near-perfect statistical fidelity (as achieved by the T3 negative control) does not imply resistance to reconstruction attacks, motivating Q-mix as the mechanism that actually suppresses reconstruction R^2 for high-risk variables like HR and glucose.
key_finding: Within the portion of the paper reviewed (Sections 1-6.3, the framework, threat model, operator definitions, and single-column geometric/distributional experiments), the paper's own numeric results are: (1) mean/variance preservation (C1) holds to essentially machine precision for all operators -- e.g. Table 1 reports max |delta mu| on the order of 1e-13 to 1e-17 and max |delta sigma| on the order of 1e-13 to 1e-16 across T1_uniform, T1_weighted, T2, and T3 in both physical and standardized space; (2) the configured l-infinity privacy bound alpha is empirically respected -- the realized perturbation never exceeds the configured alpha, and on over 99.9% of stays the realized magnitude falls in the 0.7*alpha-0.9*alpha range; (3) at alpha=0.8, T1 and T2 keep KS distance below ~0.1 for most vitals/labs (up to ~0.14-0.15 for heavy-tailed variables like creatinine and DBP), while T3 has the smallest KS distances throughout, described as behaving 'almost like a marginally isometric transform'; (4) the paper states -- as a qualitative/text-stated finding rather than a table the reviewer directly inspected -- that increasing alpha alone drives reconstruction R^2 down only gradually, and for HR and glucose specifically R^2 can remain in the 0.6-0.9 (elsewhere stated 0.8-0.9) range even at large alpha without Q-mix; adding the per-stay Q-mix wrapper at a fixed alpha=1.0 is reported to drive R^2 for HR and glucose reconstruction under L2 + linear A-family attacks down to 'close to zero.' (5) T3, despite passing all of C1-C4 and having the best statistical/marginal fidelity of the three operators, is explicitly labeled a negative case because it 'remains highly invertible under L2 combined with A-family linear reconstruction attacks, with R2 close to 1' -- the paper's central empirical argument that statistical similarity alone is not evidence of privacy.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution (not related work) is a column-wise geometric transformation framework for privacy-preserving sharing of structured EHR/ICU time-series data that stays both usable AND visible (i.e., humans can still directly inspect the transformed numeric tables), unlike MPC/HE/TEE/DP-synthetic approaches which the authors place in a 'usable but invisible' or 'synthetic but DP' quadrant. They formalize a 'mean-variance manifold' (each standardized column lies on M(0,1) = zero-mean hyperplane intersected with a fixed-norm sphere) and define four design constraints C1-C4 (exact mean/variance preservation, a unified z-score l-infinity privacy knob alpha, 'full variability' so most points actually move, and O(n)/O(n log n) CPU-only complexity) plus a threat model C5. On top of this they introduce three concrete column-wise operators -- T1 (local triplet rotation), T2 (noise-plus-manifold-projection), and T3 (global Householder reflection, explicitly kept only as a negative control) -- and a per-stay orthogonal 'Q-mix' extension for a small set of high-risk variables (e.g., heart rate, glucose). The operators, the C1-C5 formalization, the Q-mix extension, and the described three-layer 'EHR-Privacy-Agent' deployment system (operators/modules/skills, nightly CPU-only de-identification) are the paper's own artifacts, evaluated on a MIMIC-IV ICU subset. A second, explicitly stated contribution is methodological: a documented human-AI co-design protocol in which an AI assistant called 'SciencePal' proposes candidate operators and literature-search summaries under human-specified constraints, while the human team formally proves/refutes properties, designs and runs the attacks, and makes all final privacy claims (SciencePal is described as a 'constrained tool inventor,' not an autonomous decision-maker).
threat_model: The authors define a 'no-key, structure-aware adversary' analogous to Kerckhoffs's principle: the adversary is assumed to fully know the operator family's mathematical form, pseudo-code, and public hyperparameters (alpha, window lengths, which variables have Q-mix enabled), but does not know the per-stay/per-variable pseudo-random seeds used internally (e.g., to build the orthogonal/permutation matrix Q_{s,v}), which are one-time secrets confined to the hospital environment and never exported. They explicitly state they are not building a reversible encryption scheme or claiming IND-CPA/IND-CCA-style security -- their reconstruction/membership experiments are described as 'simplified chosen-plaintext-style evaluations' that serve as empirical lower bounds on privacy risk, not formal proofs. Attacker capability is parameterized by three leakage levels: L0 (no paired raw/transformed samples visible, e.g. external researchers), L1 (roughly 0.01% of stays/time points leaked as paired samples, e.g. accidental spot-check exposure), and L2 (roughly 20% of stays/time points leaked, modeling a stronger insider or multi-center scenario). Four attack families are defined: A = pointwise/sequence reconstruction, B = record linkage/re-identification, C = membership inference, D = attribute inference / IND-style bit-guessing games. The paper states plainly that in this work they mainly instantiate A-family (single-column linear reconstruction) attacks and use membership proxies as approximate C-family indicators, and that a full treatment of B/C/D and the multivariate setting is left to future work -- this scoping limitation is stated by the authors themselves, not inferred.
limitations: Limitations stated explicitly by the authors: (a) they scope the empirical attack evaluation mainly to A-family (reconstruction) linear attacks and C-family membership-inference proxies, and state that full B-family (record linkage) and D-family (attribute inference / IND-style) attacks, and the fully multivariate (cross-column) setting, are 'left for future work'; (b) they do not claim formal cryptographic security (no IND-CPA/IND-CCA-style guarantee) -- privacy is argued empirically via attack lower bounds under a specific 'no-key, structure-aware' adversary, not via a security proof against arbitrary adversaries; (c) the threat model explicitly assumes internal per-stay/per-variable seeds are never exposed outside the hospital pipeline -- if that operational assumption is violated (e.g., seed leakage or a weak PRNG), the stated guarantees would not hold, and the paper does not evaluate that failure mode; (d) although framed as generalizable to 'structured EHR and real-world clinical datasets in general,' the paper implements and evaluates only on ICU time-series data from a single dataset (MIMIC-IV adult ICU subset, LOS >= 24h), and the reviewer notes the extracted 22-page excerpt did not reach downstream AUROC/AUPRC task-utility tables, later reconstruction-attack tables (Section 6.7), or the appendix documenting the human/SciencePal division-of-labor in full detail -- claims about those specific numeric downstream-utility results are therefore not independently verified here beyond the qualitative statements the authors make in-text.

datasets_used: MIMIC-IV adult ICU subset (Johnson et al., 2023b), first ICU stays with LOS >= 24 hours, resampled to 1-hour grid over the first 48 hours
quantitative_results: Max |delta mu| (mean preservation, C1) on the order of 1e-13 to 1e-17 across T1_uniform, T1_weighted, T2, T3 (Table 1)
Max |delta sigma| (variance preservation, C1) on the order of 1e-13 to 1e-16 across the same operators (Table 1)
Empirical l-infinity perturbation stays below configured alpha for all operators; on >99.9% of stays realized magnitude falls in 0.7*alpha-0.9*alpha
At alpha=0.8: T1/T2 KS distance below ~0.1 for most vitals/labs, up to ~0.14-0.15 for heavy-tailed variables (creatinine, DBP); T3 has the smallest KS distances of the three operators
Reconstruction R^2 for HR/glucose reported in the 0.6-0.9 (also stated 0.8-0.9) range under increasing alpha without Q-mix, described as not showing a 'qualitative jump' in privacy
With Q-mix + T1/T2 at alpha=1.0, reconstruction R^2 for HR/glucose under L2 + linear A-family attacks reported to drop to 'close to zero'
T3 (negative control) reported to have reconstruction R^2 'close to 1' under L2 + A-family linear reconstruction attacks despite passing all geometric constraints C1-C4
baselines_compared: i.i.d. Gaussian noise in z-score space
T3 (Householder reflection) used internally as a negative-control/high-invertibility baseline against T1/T2
